CriterionMarksKey Focus

3.1
Engineering MaterialsProperties, costs, classification and supply

You must be able to identify materials from their physical appearance and describe their key properties. The five core mechanical properties are:

💪 Toughness / Brittleness

Toughness is the ability to absorb energy before fracturing. Mild steel and Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymer (CFRP) are examples of tough materials. Brittleness is the opposite — a material shatters suddenly without deforming, like cast iron or ceramics.

🪗 Ductility

Ability to be drawn out into a wire without breaking. Copper and aluminium are highly ductile, making them suitable for electrical wiring. Tested by tensile testing.

🔧 Malleability

Ability to be hammered or rolled into thin sheets without cracking. Lead and gold are very malleable. Most metals are malleable when cold, and thermoforming polymers are malleable when heated. This differs from ductility as the direction of force is compressive rather than tensile.

💎 Hardness

Resistance to surface indentation or scratching. High carbon steel is very hard, making it suitable for cutting tools. Measured using Vickers or Brinell hardness tests.

⚖️ Strength & Stiffness

Strength = force before permanent deformation. Stiffness = resistance to elastic deformation (Young's Modulus). Steel is both strong and stiff. Other forms of strength include Torsional strength (resistance to twisting forces) and Bending strength (resistance to bending or flexing).

3.1.1
Metals & AlloysFerrous, non-ferrous, and how properties change
DIAGRAM — Metal Classification Tree

Ferrous Metals Reference Table

MaterialCompositionKey PropertiesCommon UsesCan be machined?
Cast IronIron + 2–4% carbonVery hard, brittle, good in compression, cheapEngine blocks, manhole covers, cookwareYes (with difficulty)
Low Carbon (Mild) SteelIron + <0.3% CDuctile, tough, malleable, weldable, rustsCar bodies, structural frames, nuts/boltsYes, easily
High Carbon SteelIron + 0.6–1.5% CVery hard, strong, brittle, less ductileCutting tools, springs, railsDifficult — must anneal first
Stainless SteelIron + Cr (>11%) + NiCorrosion resistant, hard, tough, hygienicSurgical tools, cutlery, sinks, food equipmentYes but work-hardens

Non-Ferrous Metals Reference Table

MaterialKey PropertiesCommon UsesNotes
AluminiumLightweight, corrosion resistant, good conductor, malleableAircraft, drinks cans, window frames, foilCheap, widely used; poor strength vs steel
CopperExcellent electrical & thermal conductor, ductile, antimicrobialWiring, pipes, PCBs, heat exchangersMore expensive than aluminium
LeadVery dense, malleable, soft, toxic, low melting pointSolder, radiation shielding, batteriesRestricted in many products due to toxicity
ZincSelf-protecting oxide layer, low melting pointGalvanising steel, die casting, brass alloy componentOften used as a coating to prevent rust
Brass (Cu+Zn)Corrosion resistant, machinable, good appearance, non-sparkingPlumbing fittings, musical instruments, gearsAlloy — mix of copper and zinc
Bronze (Cu+Sn)Hard, wear resistant, good in marine environmentsBearings, marine hardware, statues, bellsAlloy — copper and tin

How Properties Change

Alloying

Adding elements disrupts the regular atomic structure — dislocations can't move as freely → harder and stronger. e.g. adding carbon to iron → steel.

Grain Size (Heating)

Slow cooling → large grains → softer, more ductile. Fast cooling → small grains → harder, stronger. Annealing allows large grains to form.

Cold Working

Deforming metal at room temp (rolling, drawing) introduces dislocations → work hardening → increases strength/hardness, reduces ductility.

Hardening & Quenching

Heat steel to austenitising temp, then quench (rapid cool in water/oil) → martensitic structure → very hard and brittle. Often followed by tempering.

Corrosion Effects

Rust (iron oxide) is flaky and non-protective. Aluminium oxide is thin and self-sealing (protective). Galvanic corrosion occurs between dissimilar metals.

Carbon in Steel

↑ Carbon → ↑ hardness, ↑ strength, ↓ ductility, ↓ weldability. Low carbon is tough and flexible. High carbon is hard and wear-resistant.

3.1.2
PolymersThermoplastics vs Thermosetting — key difference
⭐ Exam Key Point

Thermoplastics can be reheated and reshaped repeatedly (recyclable). Thermosetting polymers permanently set when heated — they CANNOT be remelted (cross-linked structure). This is the most tested distinction.

DIAGRAM — Polymer Structure Comparison

Thermoplastics

PolymerKey PropertiesUses
ABSTough, impact resistant, rigid, good surface finishLego, car interior trim, 3D printing
Acrylic (PMMA)Clear, stiff, UV resistant, scratches easilySignage, windows, lenses
Nylon (PA)Self-lubricating, wear resistant, flexibleGears, bearings, ropes, clothing
PolycarbonateVery tough, transparent, heat resistantSafety helmets, glasses lenses, CDs
PolystyreneRigid, brittle, light; expanded form = insulatorPackaging, disposable cups

Thermosetting Polymers

PolymerKey PropertiesUses
Epoxy ResinStrong adhesive, chemical resistant, rigidCircuit boards, adhesives (Araldite), GRP matrix
Polyester ResinRigid, brittle, clear or coloured, cheapGRP boats, car body filler
MelamineHard, scratch resistant, heat resistant surfaceKitchen worktops, crockery, laminates
PolyurethaneFlexible or rigid foam form, good insulatorFoam cushions, insulation, varnish coatings
Vulcanised RubberElastic, tough, wear resistant, heat resistantTyres, seals, conveyor belts
3.1.3
CompositesTwo or more materials combined for enhanced properties
Definition

A composite is made of at least two distinct materials: a matrix (binder) and a reinforcement. The resulting material has better properties than either component alone.

DIAGRAM — Fibre Reinforced Polymer (FRP) Structure
CompositeReinforcementMatrixPropertiesUses
CFRP (Carbon Fibre)Carbon fibresEpoxy resinExtremely strong and stiff per unit weight, expensiveAircraft, F1 cars, sports equipment
GRP (Glass Fibre)Glass fibresPolyester/epoxyGood strength, light, weather resistant, cheaper than CFRPBoat hulls, car body panels, bath tubs
PlywoodWood veneers (alternate grain)Adhesive glueStronger than solid timber, resists splitting, stableConstruction, furniture, packaging
MDFWood fibresResin binderSmooth, uniform, easy to machine, not moisture resistantFurniture, flat-pack products, shelving
OSBWood strands (oriented)Wax/resinStructural strength, moisture resistant, cheaper than plywoodStructural panels in construction
Structural ConcreteSteel rebarConcreteStrong in compression AND tension (rebar handles tension)Bridges, buildings, foundations

How Composite Properties Change

Fibre Direction

Aligning all fibres in one direction → strongest in that direction but weak at 90°. Cross-ply layering gives more even strength.

Matrix Type

Stiffer matrix transfers load better to fibres. Flexible matrix gives impact resistance. Epoxy gives better properties than polyester.

Amount of Reinforcement

More fibre volume fraction = stronger and stiffer, up to a limit. Too much fibre → poor matrix distribution → weaker joints.

Size & Shape of Reinforcement

Longer fibres give better strength transfer. Woven mats give 2D strength. Chopped strand mat gives random strength in all 2D directions.

3.1.4
Other Materials + Costs & SupplyTimbers, ceramics, material economics
Structural Timbers

Used in construction framing. Properties: relatively light, renewable, can be treated for moisture/fire resistance. Softwoods (pine, spruce) for framing; hardwoods (oak) for more demanding applications. Prone to rot and insect attack without treatment.

Ceramics

Very hard, brittle, high temperature resistant, chemically inert, electrical insulators. Uses: spark plugs, cutting tool tips (ceramic inserts), turbine blade coatings, tiles, bricks, glass. Cannot be easily machined once fired.

Material Costs & Supply (3.1.2)

FactorDetails
Comparative CostsTimber < Steel < Aluminium < Copper < CFRP. Rarer = more expensive. Recyclability affects cost (aluminium recycling is energy efficient)
Economies of ScaleBuying in bulk reduces cost per unit — "price breaks". A manufacturer ordering 10,000 steel bars pays much less per bar than ordering 10.
Stock SizesMaterials come in standard sizes (sheet, bar, tube, angle). Non-standard sizes cost more and create more waste. Design should use standard sizes.
WasteSubtractive manufacturing wastes material. Additive manufacturing (3D printing) minimises waste. Waste = extra cost and environmental impact.
RecyclabilityMetals and thermoplastics are generally recyclable. Thermosets cannot be recycled. Composites are difficult to recycle — end of life concern.
AvailabilitySome materials (rare earth metals, titanium) have limited supply → price rises. Supply chain disruptions affect availability.

Factors Influencing Design (3.1.3)

Energy Production
  • Wind — renewable, no emissions, visual impact
  • Solar — clean, high capital cost, weather dependent
  • Tidal — predictable, limited locations, high cost
  • Nuclear — reliable, large output, waste problem
  • Fossil fuels — cheap, reliable, CO₂ emissions
  • Biomass — renewable but can compete with food
Engineered Lifespans

Planned obsolescence — products designed to fail or become obsolete. Sealed parts prevent maintenance, reducing lifespan. Maintenance ensures safety and efficiency — lubrication, anti-corrosion, wear compensation, EOL disposal.

3.2
Engineering Manufacturing ProcessesKnow which process suits which material
DIAGRAM — Manufacturing Process Categories
ADDITIVE
Build up layers
→
SUBTRACTIVE
Remove material
→
DEFORMING
Change shape
→
CASTING/MOULDING
Liquid to solid
→
JOINING
Combine parts
3.2.1
Additive Manufacturing3D printing — building layer by layer
Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)

Thermoplastic filament (e.g. PLA, ABS) melted and deposited layer by layer. Most common 3D printing. Good for prototypes and functional parts. Limited surface finish.

POLYMERS PROTOTYPE

Sintering (SLS / SLM)

Laser fuses powder (metal or polymer) layer by layer. Selective Laser Melting (SLM) for metals — creates dense, high-strength metal parts. Used in aerospace and medical implants.

METALS HIGH VALUE

Rapid Prototyping (Polymers)

Includes SLA (stereolithography — UV-cured resin) and PolyJet. Very high detail and smooth surfaces. Used for aesthetic prototypes, dental moulds, hearing aids.

POLYMERS FINE DETAIL

Advantages of Additive Manufacturing

Near-zero material waste · Can produce complex internal geometries impossible by other methods · No tooling required · Rapid — design to part in hours · Customisable (e.g. medical implants) · Ideal for one-offs and small batches

Disadvantages

Slow for mass production · Layer lines visible (surface finish) · Limited material range vs traditional · Polymer parts weaker than moulded/machined equivalents · Expensive for large metal parts

3.2.2
Material Removal (Subtractive)Cutting, turning, milling, drilling, etching
ProcessHow it worksMaterialsKey points
SawingToothed blade removes thin strip of material (kerf)Metal, wood, plasticHacksaw (manual), band saw, circular saw. CNC variants available.
ShearingTwo blades cut sheet material like scissorsSheet metalNo heat generated. Used for straight cuts on thin sheet.
Laser CuttingFocused laser beam vaporises/melts materialMetal, plastic, wood, acrylicVery precise, narrow kerf, no tool contact, CNC controlled. Heat affected zone.
Turning (Lathe)Workpiece rotates; cutting tool moves linearlyMetals, plasticsCylindrical: straight bar. Tapered: angled. Boring: enlarging hole. Uses spindle speed formulas.
MillingRotating multi-tooth cutter removes material from stationary workpieceMetals, plastics, woodFace milling: flat surfaces. Slot milling: grooves/slots. CNC milling very precise.
DrillingRotating drill bit creates holesMost materialsPillar drill for accuracy. Centre drilling in lathe for accurate start. Spindle speed depends on material and drill diameter.
Chemical EtchingAcid dissolves unprotected areasCopper (PCBs), metalsPCB: copper board coated in photo-resist; UV exposes pattern; etched in ferric chloride. Alternative: CNC PCB routing.

Parts of a Centre Lathe

DIAGRAM — Centre Lathe (labelled photograph)

Key Lathe Parts — What Each Does

PartFunctionExam tip
Lathe BedHeavy cast iron base. Precision-ground slideways (ways) on top guide all moving parts in perfect alignment.Made of cast iron — rigid and vibration-damping
HeadstockFixed at the left end. Houses the spindle, gearbox and speed-change levers. Transfers rotation to the chuck.Gear levers on headstock change spindle speed (RPM)
ChuckClamps the workpiece. 3-jaw = self-centering (all jaws move together) — for round/hex bar. 4-jaw = independent jaws for off-centre or square work.3-jaw = fast, self-centering; 4-jaw = versatile but slower to set
ToolpostClamps the cutting tool at exactly centre height. Four-way toolpost holds up to 4 tools — indexed quickly between operations.Tool tip must be exactly on the workpiece centre line
Top Slide (Compound Rest)Mounted on cross slide. Can be swivelled to any angle for taper turning and fine facing cuts.Set at angle = taper turning. Set parallel = fine feed facing
Cross SlideMoves the tool toward/away from the workpiece (radial direction). Controls the depth of cut and the final diameter.Wind in = reduces diameter; wind out = increases diameter
Saddle (Carriage)Saddle sits on the bed ways and slides left/right along the bed. Saddle handle controls manual longitudinal movement.Saddle moves tool along the length of the work — produces length
LeadscrewPrecision threaded rod along the front of the bed. Carriage split-nut engages it at a precise ratio to spindle speed for screw-cutting.Leadscrew = thread cutting ONLY. Feedshaft = normal turning feeds
FeedshaftSmooth rod (with keyway) running parallel to leadscrew. Used for automatic power feed during normal turning — does not cut threads.Feedshaft drives carriage/cross slide for turning operations
Coolant SupplyPump delivers cutting fluid to the cutting zone. Reduces heat, lubricates the tool/workpiece interface, flushes chips away.Keeps tool sharp longer; improves surface finish
TailstockSlides along bed ways and locks anywhere. Holds a dead/live centre to support the right-hand end of long work. Also holds a drill chuck for drilling on the lathe.Always support work longer than 3–4× its diameter with tailstock centre
Emergency StopLarge red mushroom button. Cuts all power immediately. Must be accessible and tested before each session.H&S — always know the location of the E-stop before starting
Motor LeverEngages/disengages the drive motor. Allows spindle to be stopped without switching off the machine.Different from E-stop — normal operational control
Spindle Speed Calculation (M2.3) — you may be asked to calculate this

N (RPM) = (Cutting Speed × 1000) ÷ (π × Diameter)
Cutting speed in m/min; diameter in mm. Faster speeds suit soft materials (aluminium, brass). Slower for hard materials (high carbon steel, stainless) and larger diameters. Too fast = tool overheats and fails. Too slow = poor productivity.

N (RPM) = (Cutting Speed × 1000) ÷ (π × Diameter) — where cutting speed is in m/min and diameter in mm. Faster speeds for soft materials (aluminium); slower for hard materials (steel) and larger diameters. Too fast → tool overheats and fails. Too slow → poor productivity.

Parts of a Horizontal Milling Machine

DIAGRAM — Horizontal Milling Machine (labelled)
PartFunctionKey detail
BaseHeavy cast iron foundation. Supports the entire machine and contains the coolant reservoir.Must be rigid to prevent vibration during cutting
ColumnVertical casting bolted to the base. Houses the spindle drive gearbox and motor. The knee slides up and down its face.Main structural member — all other parts attach to it
KneeSlides up and down the column face on vertical ways. Supports the saddle and table. Vertical adjustment controls depth of cut.Knee raised/lowered = vertical (Z) axis movement
SaddleSits on top of the knee. Slides in/out (toward/away from column). Controls cross-feed (Y axis).Saddle movement = Y axis (cross feed)
TableFlat cast iron work surface with T-slots for clamping workpieces/vices. Travels left-right along the saddle (X axis — longitudinal feed).Table movement = X axis (longitudinal feed). T-slots accept T-bolts for workholding.
ArborHorizontal shaft extending from the spindle. Milling cutters (slab mills, side-and-face cutters) are mounted on it.Key difference from vertical mill — cutter rotates about horizontal axis
Arbor SupportBearing support at the far end of the arbor. Prevents the arbor flexing or vibrating during heavy cuts.Must be fitted whenever a long arbor is used
Overhanging ArmExtends horizontally from the top of the column over the table. The arbor support slides along it to any position.Can be adjusted to suit different arbor lengths
Horizontal vs Vertical Milling

Horizontal (shown above): cutter spins on horizontal arbor. Best for heavy slab milling, side-and-face cuts, gang milling. High material removal rate.

Vertical: cutter spins on vertical spindle (like a drill). Better for face milling, end milling, pocket milling, slot cutting. More versatile — used more commonly in schools.

Milling Operations (AQA spec)
  • Face milling — cutter face produces flat surface
  • Slot milling — side-and-face cutter cuts groove/slot
  • Workpiece held in machine vice bolted to table
  • CNC milling: all 3 axes controlled by G-code
  • Spindle speed (RPM) = cutting speed × 1000 ÷ (π × cutter dia.)
3.2.3
Shaping — Forming & ManipulationBending, pressing, punching, composites
Bending / Folding

Sheet metal bent along a straight line using a folder/press brake. Used for brackets, enclosures. Springback must be accounted for — material tries to return to original shape.

Press Forming

Sheet metal pressed between male and female die under high force. Produces 3D shapes — car body panels, kitchen sinks. Very fast for high volume production.

Composite Lay-up

Layers of fibre mat/cloth wetted with resin, laid into a mould. Cured by hand (wet lay-up) or in an autoclave under heat and pressure (aerospace quality). Resin infusion (VARTM) improves consistency.

Punching

Die and punch cut holes or blanks from sheet metal. Blanking removes the outer shape; punching creates holes. Very fast — used for mass production of sheet metal parts.

Stamping

Sheet metal is shaped in a die under impact/press force. Combination of punching, blanking and forming in one stroke. Used for coins, electrical contacts, small components at high volume.

Composite Lay-up (Wet Lay-up / Hand Lay-up)

DIAGRAM — Wet Lay-up Composite Moulding Process
Part / StepDetail
Mould ToolFemale mould (concave) defining the outer shape. Usually GRP, wood or aluminium. Must be coated with release agent first so the part can be removed.
Gel Coat (optional)Pigmented resin applied first to the mould surface. Becomes the outer cosmetic layer of the finished part — smooth and weather resistant.
Dry Reinforcement FabricGlass fibre mat (CSM), woven cloth or carbon fibre fabric. Laid into the mould in layers. Provides the structural strength.
ResinPolyester or epoxy resin (with hardener/catalyst) applied by brush or roller over each layer of fabric. Wets out the fibres and bonds layers together.
Consolidation RollerRidged metal roller used to press each layer into the mould, work out trapped air bubbles and ensure full wet-out of the fibres.
CuringResin cures (sets permanently) at room temperature via chemical reaction (addition of catalyst). Higher temperatures accelerate cure. Autoclave curing (heat + pressure) gives aerospace-quality laminates.
Key Points — Wet Lay-up vs Autoclave

Wet lay-up: low cost, low equipment needed, manual process, good for one-offs and large parts (boat hulls, car body panels). Variable quality depends on worker skill. Autoclave: heat + pressure applied after lay-up — removes voids, gives consistent high-fibre-volume fraction — used in aerospace CFRP parts. Much more expensive.

3.2.4
Casting & MouldingLiquid metal/polymer solidified in a mould

Sand Casting — Parts of the Mould (6-step process)

DIAGRAM — Sand Casting Process Steps (labelled)
Part / TermWhat it isWhy it matters
DragLower half of the moulding flask (box frame). The first half filled with green sand around the bottom of the pattern.Sits on the moulding board — stays in place throughout. Runner and gate cut at its top surface.
CopeUpper half of the flask, placed on top of the drag once the drag is complete and the pattern split.Contains the sprue hole, riser and vent impressions. Lifted off to remove pattern.
Flat-sided split patternA model of the finished casting, split in two along the parting line. Each half sits in one half of the flask.Must have draft angles (1–3°) so it pulls cleanly from the sand without destroying the mould wall.
Strickling offLevelling the sand flush with the top of the drag using a straight bar after ramming.Ensures the drag has a flat, level parting surface for the cope to sit on.
Sprue pinTapered wooden/metal rod placed in the cope before ramming. Pulled out to leave the sprue channel.Creates the vertical channel down which molten metal flows into the mould.
Parting lineThe horizontal join between cope and drag — the split line of the pattern.Runner and gate channels are cut along this line after the pattern is removed.
Mould cavityThe void left in the sand after the pattern is withdrawn from both halves.This is the exact shape of the finished casting. Quality of finish depends on sand fineness and pattern smoothness.
Pouring basinFunnel-shaped depression formed at the top of the sprue to receive the ladle of metal.Reduces turbulence at the sprue entrance. Should be kept full during pouring to maintain head pressure.
Sprue (hole)Vertical tapered channel from pouring basin down to the runner at the parting line.Tapered — wide at top, narrow at base — to prevent low-pressure zones that would suck in air.
RunnerHorizontal channel cut at the parting line connecting the sprue base to the gate(s).Slows metal flow and traps slag (slag floats — stays in runner, clean metal enters cavity).
GateSmall opening cut from runner into the mould cavity. Controls rate of metal entry.Narrower than runner — keeps runner full to prevent air ingestion. Multiple gates for large castings.
RiserVertical reservoir column connected to the top/end of the mould cavity.Acts as a molten metal reservoir — feeds cavity as casting shrinks during solidification. Also shows mould is full.
VentsThin holes poked with wire down through the cope sand above the cavity.Allow trapped air and hot gases to escape during pouring — prevents gas porosity defects.
Green sandMoist, clay-bonded silica sand. Not green in colour — "green" means unbaked/moist.Can be rammed tightly, holds shape well, is permeable to gas, and is fully reusable.

Pressure Die Casting — Parts of the Machine

DIAGRAM — Cold Chamber Pressure Die Casting Machine (labelled)
PartFunctionKey detail
Fixed PlatenRigid frame member that the fixed die half bolts to. Does not move during operation.Contains the sprue bush — the entry point from the shot chamber into the die.
Moving PlatenSlides on tie bars toward and away from fixed platen to open and close the die.Driven by a large hydraulic clamping cylinder at the rear of the machine.
Die (Die Halves)Two-part hardened tool steel mould. When clamped shut, the internal cavity defines the shape of the casting.Machined to high precision — surface quality of die = surface finish of casting. Lasts 100,000+ shots.
Casting CavityThe hollow space between the closed die halves that molten metal fills under pressure.Can include cores to create holes. Runner and gate channels machined into die faces direct metal in.
Chamber (Shot Sleeve)Horizontal cylinder attached to fixed platen. A measured amount (shot) of molten metal is ladled in through the pouring hole on top.Called "cold chamber" because it is separate from the furnace — metal is ladled in each shot. Used for aluminium, magnesium.
Injection Piston (Plunger)Hydraulically-driven piston inside the shot sleeve. Pushes molten metal into the die cavity at high speed and pressure.Two-phase stroke: slow first phase purges air from sleeve; rapid second phase forces metal into cavity in milliseconds at 40–160 MPa.
Metal LadleUsed to transfer a precise volume of molten metal from the holding furnace into the shot chamber for each cycle.Automated ladles used in production — ensures consistent shot weight, reducing defects.
Ejector PinsHardened rods set into the moving die half. Fire forward when die opens to push the solidified casting off the die face.Leave small circular witness marks on casting — positioned in non-critical areas. Must retract before next shot.
Cooling ChannelsWater/oil circuits drilled through both die halves to extract heat and control solidification rate.Die maintained at 120–280°C depending on alloy. Too cold = misrun defect; too hot = long cycle time and die erosion.
Tie BarsFour large steel bars linking fixed and moving platens. Carry the clamping force in tension during injection.Clamping force must exceed injection force — otherwise die opens during fill, causing flash defect.
Pressure Die Casting — The 7-Step Cycle (know this sequence!)

1. Die closes — moving platen advances, die halves lock under clamping force
2. Metal ladled in — measured shot of molten metal poured into shot chamber
3. Injection — piston advances slowly first (purges air), then accelerates rapidly — metal fills cavity in milliseconds at 40–160 MPa
4. Solidification — high pressure held while metal cools. Cooling channels extract heat.
5. Die opens — moving platen retracts, casting stays on moving half
6. Ejection — ejector pins fire, pushing casting clear of die
7. Die spray — release agent/lubricant sprayed on die faces, then cycle repeats (cycle time: 15–60 seconds)

Injection Moulding — Parts of the Machine

DIAGRAM — Injection Moulding Machine (labelled)
PartFunction
HopperFunnel at top — holds raw thermoplastic granules/pellets. Gravity-feeds material into the barrel.
Heater BandsElectrical heaters clamped around the barrel in zones. Melt the granules progressively (typically 180–300°C depending on polymer).
Screw (Reciprocating)Rotating screw inside barrel. Mixes and melts the plastic, and conveys it forward toward the mould. Then acts as a plunger to inject the shot.
BarrelLong steel cylinder housing the screw. Contains the heater zones. Must withstand injection pressures.
Mould (Die)Two-part hardened steel tool. Defines the shape of the finished part. Cooled by water channels to solidify the plastic quickly.
Ram (Hydraulic)Hydraulic cylinder that drives the screw forward during injection, forcing molten plastic into the mould at high pressure.
Hydraulic Fluid SystemProvides power to the ram (injection) and clamping unit. High-pressure oil circuit controlled precisely.
MotorElectric motor that rotates the screw during the plasticising (melting) phase.
NozzleTip of barrel — connects to sprue bush on mould. Directs molten polymer into mould runner system.
Injection Moulding — Key Facts

Only works with thermoplastics (not thermosets — they would cure in the barrel). Very high volume production — cycle time as low as 10 seconds. High tooling cost but very low cost per part at volume. Near net shape — minimal waste. Parts eject automatically via ejector pins. Typical materials: ABS, nylon, polypropylene, polycarbonate.

3.2.5
Joining & AssemblyPermanent and temporary fastening methods
Key Distinction

Permanent joints cannot be undone without damage (welding, riveting, soldering). Temporary joints can be disassembled (nuts/bolts, screws).

MethodTypeProcessUses & Notes
RivetsPermanentMetal pin deformed to clamp sheets together. Pop rivets for one-sided access.Aircraft skins, structural steel, signage. Shear strength is key.
Threaded FasteningsTemporaryBolt + nut or machine screw into tapped hole. Tightened by torque.Most versatile — allows disassembly for maintenance. Use locking nut for vibration.
Soft SolderingPermanentFiller metal (Sn-Pb or Sn-Cu) melted below 450°C using soldering iron. Flux cleans surfaces.Electronic PCB joints. Low strength joint — NOT structural.
Hard Soldering (Silver)PermanentSilver-based filler above 450°C, uses flux, torch required.Jewellery, copper pipe, dental work. Stronger than soft solder.
BrazingPermanentBrass-based filler at 700–900°C using flux and torch or furnace.Stronger than soldering. Joins dissimilar metals. Bike frames.
Welding (MIG/TIG/Arc)PermanentBase metal melted and fused with filler. Very high temperatures (1500°C+).Strongest joint — can exceed base metal strength. Requires PPE. MIG for speed, TIG for precision.
3.2.6
Heat & Chemical TreatmentChanging metal properties with heat
DIAGRAM — Heat Treatment Temperature and Effect
ProcessMethodEffect on SteelWhy Used?
NormalisingHeat above critical temp → air coolUniform, fine grain structure. Improved toughness vs work-hardened state.After welding or machining to relieve stresses and refine grain
AnnealingHeat above critical temp → very slow furnace coolLarge grain structure, very soft and ductile, internal stresses removedBefore machining to make material easier to cut; after cold working
HardeningHeat to austenitising temp → rapid quench (water/oil)Martensitic structure — very hard but brittleCutting tools, dies — need hardness at working surface
QuenchingRapid cooling from high temperatureTraps carbon, creates martensite, extreme hardnessPart of hardening process — must then temper to reduce brittleness
TemperingRe-heat hardened steel to 200–300°C → air coolReduces brittleness while retaining most hardness. Temper colour indicates temperature: light straw (220°C) → brown (260°C) → blue (300°C)After hardening — makes tools tough enough to withstand impact without shattering
Case HardeningHeat mild steel in carbon-rich environment (carburising) → quenchHard, wear-resistant outer case with tough, ductile coreGears, camshafts — need hard surface but must not be brittle throughout
3.2.7
Surface FinishingProtection, aesthetics, and conductivity
Painting

Liquid applied by brush, roller or spray. Primer first for adhesion. Topcoat for colour/protection. Cheap but can chip/peel. Powder coating (electrostatic) more durable.

Dip Coating (Plastisol)

Metal heated and dipped in powdered polymer. Powder fuses to surface. Creates soft, colourful, corrosion-resistant coating. Used for tool handles, dishwasher baskets.

Electroplating

Metal object as cathode in electrolytic bath. Positive metal ions (e.g. chromium, gold, nickel) deposited on surface. Improves appearance and corrosion resistance. Used for car trim, jewellery, taps.

Galvanising

Steel coated with zinc — either by hot dipping or electroplating. Zinc corrodes preferentially (sacrificial protection), protecting the steel. Used for fence posts, crash barriers, guttering.

Polishing

Abrasive removes surface peaks, increasing reflectivity and smoothness. Reduces surface roughness (Ra value). Improves fatigue resistance by removing surface cracks. Used for bearings, optical surfaces.

3.3
SystemsMechanical, Electrical, Electronic, Structural, Pneumatic
DIAGRAM — Basic Systems Block Diagram
INPUT
Sensor, switch, signal
→
PROCESS
Controller, logic, gear
→
OUTPUT
Motor, lamp, alarm
↑ Feedback loop: output signal compared to desired output → adjusts process (e.g. thermostat)
Systems Representations

You must know: System block diagrams (input → process → output + feedback) · Schematic drawings (electrical/pneumatic circuit diagrams using standard symbols) · Flow charts (decision-making in programs)

3.3.1
Mechanical SystemsGears, linkages, cams, pulleys, bearings

Motion Conversion

Rotary → Reciprocating

Crank and slider mechanism (e.g. engine piston). Cam and follower — cam rotates, follower moves up/down. Used in engines, pumps, sewing machines.

Rotary → Oscillating

Crank and rocker linkage — crank rotates, output rocks back and forth. Used in windscreen wipers, rocking mechanisms.

Linear → Rotary

Rack and pinion — linear rack meshes with circular pinion gear. Converts linear motion to rotation or vice versa. Used in car steering, machine tool feeds.

Gears & Gear Trains

DIAGRAM — Gear Train (Driver and Driven)
E14 — Gear Ratio
Gear Ratio = Number of teeth on DRIVEN gear ÷ Number of teeth on DRIVER gear
If Gear Ratio > 1 → output slower, more torque | If < 1 → output faster, less torque
E15 — Mechanical Advantage
MA = Load (Fb) ÷ Effort (Fa)
MA > 1 = machine multiplies force (e.g. hydraulic jack, lever). MA < 1 = machine multiplies speed.

Cams & Followers

DIAGRAM — Cam Types & Followers
How a Cam & Follower Works

A cam is a shaped rotating disc mounted on a shaft. As it rotates, its profile pushes against a follower, converting rotary motion into reciprocating (linear) motion. The shape of the cam determines the motion pattern of the follower — including the speed, distance (stroke), and whether there is a dwell (pause) period.

Cam Profiles & Timing

The cam profile is the outer edge shape. It controls when, how fast, and how far the follower moves. In an engine, the camshaft uses multiple cams to open and close intake and exhaust valves with precise timing. The dwell angle is the portion of rotation where the follower stays stationary.

Exam Tip

You must be able to identify cam types from their shape and describe the motion they produce. Remember: Pear = dwell, Eccentric = smooth sinusoidal, Snail = gradual rise + sudden drop. Always state the type of follower and explain how the cam converts rotary → reciprocating motion.

Pulleys

Single Fixed Pulley

Changes direction of force only. Mechanical Advantage = 1. Load = Effort (ignoring friction). Used to redirect a rope over an edge.

Multiple / Block and Tackle

Multiple pulleys reduce effort needed to lift load. MA = number of rope sections supporting load. Trade-off: must pull rope further. Used in cranes, rigging, lifts.

Bearings

Ball Bearings

Steel balls between inner and outer race. Reduce friction for rotating shafts. Handle radial and axial loads. Used in wheels, motors, fans.

Roller Bearings

Cylindrical rollers instead of balls. Higher radial load capacity. Used in gearboxes, wheel hubs where heavy loads expected.

Plain Bearings (Bush)

Simple bronze or polymer sleeve around shaft. Low cost, quiet, no rolling elements. Requires lubrication. Used in small motors, hinges.

Linkages

LinkageFunctionExample Use
Reverse MotionInput and output move in opposite directionsScissors, tongs, pliers
ParallelOutput moves in same direction as input, maintains angleCar bonnet mechanism, pantograph
Bell CrankChanges direction of motion by 90°Bicycle brakes, automotive throttle linkage
Four-bar LinkageComplex planar motion from rotationCar suspension, robotic arms
3.3.2
Electrical SystemsPower supplies, switches, AC/DC, output devices
AC vs DC — Key Difference

AC (Alternating Current) — current direction reverses periodically. UK mains = 230V, 50Hz. Used for power distribution (transformer compatible). DC (Direct Current) — current flows in one direction only. Batteries, electronics. Rectifiers convert AC → DC.

E12 — Ohm's Law
I = V ÷ R
I = current (Amps, A) · V = voltage (Volts, V) · R = resistance (Ohms, Ω)
E13 — Series Resistance
R_total = R₁ + R₂ + R₃ ...
Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ (total R < smallest R in parallel)
DIAGRAM — Series vs Parallel Circuits

Electrical Components

ComponentFunctionSymbol note
Battery/CellDC power source — converts chemical → electrical energyLong thin line (positive) + short thick line (negative)
SwitchBreaks or completes a circuit. SPST most basic.Gap in line with lever symbol
RelayElectrically operated switch — low current controls high current circuitCoil + switch symbol
MotorConverts electrical energy → rotary mechanical energyCircle with M
Buzzer/BellConverts electrical energy → soundSquare wave symbol
LampConverts electrical energy → light and heatCircle with X
SolenoidConverts electrical → linear mechanical motion (plunger)Coil with arrow
3.3.3
Electronic SystemsSensors, logic gates, microcontrollers, components

Analogue vs Digital Signals

Analogue Signal

Continuously varying signal that can take any value. e.g. temperature sensor output, audio waveform. Represented as a smooth wave.

Digital Signal

Only two states: HIGH (1) and LOW (0). More resistant to noise. Used in logic gates, microcontrollers, computers. ADC (Analogue to Digital Converter) converts between them.

Logic Gates — Truth Tables

DIAGRAM — AND, OR, NOT Gates with Truth Tables

Electronic Components

ComponentFunctionKey Notes
Resistor (fixed)Limits current flow, drops voltageRead using colour code bands. R in Ω, kΩ, MΩ.
Resistor (variable/potentiometer)Adjustable resistance — acts as voltage dividerUsed as volume controls, position sensors
CapacitorStores charge, filters signals, timing circuitsPolarised = electrolytic (observe polarity!). Non-polarised = ceramic
DiodeAllows current in one direction only (forward biased)Signal diode for small signals; rectifying diode for power
LEDEmits light when forward biased. ~2V forward voltageAlways needs series resistor to limit current. R=(Vsupply−VLED)/I
Transistor (BJT)Current-controlled switch/amplifier — small base current controls large collector currentUsed as driver for motors, buzzers. NPN most common.
FETVoltage-controlled switch — gate voltage controls drain-source currentMOSFET used for high current loads from microcontrollers
7-segment display7 LEDs arranged to show digits 0–9Common anode or cathode. Decoder IC or MCU controls which segments light.
Timer IC (555)Generates timing pulses. Monostable or astable mode.Astable = continuous square wave. Monostable = single timed pulse.
Microcontroller (PIC)Programmable chip — replaces many discrete componentsProgrammed via flowchart/code. Can handle sensors, logic, timing, output control.

Flowchart for Programming

DIAGRAM — Basic Flowchart Symbols & Example Program

Flowchart Symbols

Example: Temperature Monitor Program

3.3.4
Structural SystemsLoads, forces, space frames, monocoques

Types of Loads

Static (Dead) Load

Constant load that doesn't change — weight of the structure itself, fixed equipment. e.g. weight of a bridge deck. Acts continuously.

Dynamic (Live) Load

Variable load that changes over time — vehicles, people, wind. e.g. cars on a bridge, snow loading. Must consider peak loads in design.

Imposed Load

Load applied by external source — the use of the structure. Includes live loads plus impact loads (sudden forces). Wind and earthquake are dynamic imposed loads.

DIAGRAM — Internal Forces in a Beam
DIAGRAM — Types of Structures
Space Frame

A 3D lattice framework of triangulated members (struts and ties). Each member is in pure tension or compression (no bending). Very strong and stiff for its low weight. Used in: Cranes, roof trusses, pylons, and tubular car chassis. The triangle is the only rigid 2D shape, so all space frames rely on triangulation.

Monocoque

From French meaning "single shell". The outer skin carries ALL structural loads (it is a stressed skin). There is no internal framework. It provides excellent strength-to-weight ratio and maximizes internal space. Used in: Egg shells, ping pong balls, Formula 1 chassis (carbon fibre tub).

Semi-Monocoque

A hybrid structure where the loads are shared between an outer stressed skin and an internal substructure (formers/ribs and longitudinal stringers). This prevents the thin outer skin from buckling under heavy loads. Used in: Most modern aircraft fuselages and modern car bodies (unibody design).

Bending and Torsion

Bending

Force applied perpendicular to beam axis. Top surface = compression. Bottom surface = tension. Neutral axis = zero stress. I-beams efficient — material at flange where stress is highest.

Torsion/Buckling

Torsion = twisting force (torque). Buckling = sudden collapse of slender column under compressive load. Hollow tubes resist torsion efficiently. Increasing section thickness or bracing resists buckling.

3.3.5
Pneumatic & Hydraulic SystemsCompressed air and fluid power
E10 — Pressure
P = F ÷ A
P = Pressure (Pa or N/m²) · F = Force (N) · A = Area (m²)
FeaturePneumatic (Air)Hydraulic (Oil/Fluid)
Working fluidCompressed airIncompressible liquid (oil)
CompressibilityAir is compressible → spongy feelLiquid nearly incompressible → precise, stiff
Forces producedLower forces — typically up to a few tonnesVery high forces — thousands of tonnes possible
SpeedFast, good for rapid cyclingSlower but more controllable
CleanlinessClean (uses air)Risk of fluid leaks/contamination
UsesFactory automation, robotic grippers, spray painting, dentist drillsJCB diggers, car brakes, aircraft landing gear, presses
DIAGRAM — Basic Pneumatic Circuit
3.4
Testing & InvestigationModelling, testing methods, QC, aerodynamics

Destructive vs Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)

💥 Destructive Testing
  • Tensile test — pulls until fracture. Gives UTS, yield stress, elongation
  • Charpy impact test — measures toughness (energy absorbed)
  • Hardness test (Vickers/Brinell) — indent surface with known force
  • Compressive test — crush sample to find compressive strength

Sample destroyed — cannot be used in service. Used to qualify material/process.

✅ Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)
  • Visual inspection — surface cracks, corrosion, defects
  • Ultrasonic testing — sound waves find internal flaws
  • Dye penetrant — liquid seeps into cracks, revealed by developer
  • X-ray / radiography — reveals internal structure and voids
  • Magnetic particle — surface/near-surface cracks in ferrous metals

Component remains usable — used for in-service inspection of aircraft, welds, pressure vessels.

Quality Control Methods

MethodPurpose
TolerancesAcceptable range of variation from nominal dimension (±0.05mm). Tighter tolerances = more expensive to produce but better fit.
Vernier CalipersMeasure external/internal dimensions and depth to 0.02mm precision
MicrometerMeasure external dimensions to 0.01mm — more precise than vernier
Depth GaugeMeasure depth of holes, slots and recesses
Go/No-Go GaugeQuick pass/fail check — "Go" end must fit; "No-Go" end must NOT fit
SPC (Statistical Process Control)Statistical sampling to monitor production — spots trends before out-of-spec parts are made
CNC/CAM Software QCVirtual verification of toolpaths before machining. Simulation catches errors before material is wasted.

Aerodynamics

⬆️ Lift

Upward force on an aerofoil — low pressure above (fast flow) and high pressure below (slow flow) = pressure difference. Acts perpendicular to airflow. Bernoulli's principle.

⬅️ Drag

Force opposing motion — acts parallel to airflow. Two types: form drag (shape resistance) and skin friction drag. Streamlining reduces form drag. Affects any object moving through air/fluid.

➡️ Thrust

Force that propels object forward — produced by engine (jet, propeller, rocket). Must overcome drag to maintain speed. Net force = Thrust − Drag. If Thrust = Drag, constant velocity.

DIAGRAM — Aerofoil & Lift Generation
Aerofoil Design

An aerofoil is a specialized cross-sectional shape designed to generate lift. It features a curved (cambered) upper surface and a flatter lower surface, with a rounded leading edge and a sharp trailing edge.

Bernoulli's Principle

As air travels over the curved upper surface, it must move faster to cover the greater distance in the same time as the air moving underneath. Faster moving air exerts less pressure, creating a pressure differential that results in upward lift.

Angle of Attack

The angle between the aerofoil's chord line and the oncoming airflow. Increasing the angle increases lift up to a critical point, after which the airflow separates from the surface, causing a sudden loss of lift known as a stall.

Exam Context — Aerodynamics

You may be asked about aerodynamic terms in the context of a compressed air powered dragster — identifying which forces act on it, how streamlining reduces drag, and how design changes affect performance. In motorsports, inverted aerofoils (spoilers) are used to generate downforce, pushing the vehicle into the track for better grip.

3.4M
Key Formulae & CalculationsAll equations from Appendix 1 — must know these!
E1 — Area of Rectangle
A = L × W
Units: m², mm², cm²
E2 — Volume of Cuboid
V = L × W × H
Units: m³, mm³, cm³
E3 — Area of Circle
A = π r²
r = radius; π ≈ 3.14159
E4 — Volume of Cylinder
V = π r² × L
L = length/height of cylinder
E5 — Area of Triangle
A = ½ × B × H
B = base; H = perpendicular height
E6 — Density
ρ = m ÷ V
ρ = kg/m³, m = kg, V = m³
E7 — Stress
σ = F ÷ A
σ = Pa (N/m²); F = N; A = m²
E8 — Strain
ε = δl ÷ l
No units (ratio). δl = extension; l = original length
E9 — Young's Modulus
E = σ ÷ ε
E = Pa or GPa. High E = stiff material. Steel ≈ 200 GPa
E10 — Pressure
P = F ÷ A
P = Pa; F = N; A = m². Same equation as stress but different context.
E11 — Factor of Safety
FoS = σ_yield ÷ Working Stress
FoS > 1 = safe margin. Bridges often FoS = 4 or more.
E12 — Ohm's Law
I = V ÷ R
I = A; V = Volts; R = Ω. Rearrange: V=IR, R=V/I
E13 — Series Resistance
R_t = R₁ + R₂ + ...
Parallel: 1/R_t = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂
E14 — Gear Ratio
GR = Driven teeth ÷ Driver teeth
Output speed = Input speed ÷ GR
E15 — Mechanical Advantage
MA = Load ÷ Effort
MA > 1 = force multiplier. Think levers, pulleys, gears.

Stress-Strain Diagram

DIAGRAM — Stress-Strain Curve for Mild Steel
3.5
The Impact of Modern TechnologiesNew tech, engineering industries, society & environment
Exam requirement

You must be able to describe and discuss examples of new technologies and their impacts on production, society, and the environment. Always give specific examples.

TechnologyImpact on ProductionImpact on SocietyEnvironmental Impact
3D Printing / Additive ManufacturingRapid prototyping; custom parts; reduced waste; no tooling needed; on-demand manufacturingPersonalised medical devices; accessible design; home manufacturingReduced waste vs subtractive; polymer waste; energy use of high-temp processes
Robotics & AutomationConsistent quality; 24/7 production; reduced labour costs; higher throughputJob displacement; safer working (removes humans from hazardous tasks); cheaper productsReduced material waste; energy consumption of robots
AI & Machine LearningPredictive maintenance; quality inspection by vision systems; optimised design (generative design)Smart products; autonomous vehicles; medical diagnosis; changed skill requirementsOptimises energy use in factories; significant data centre energy demand
Smart MaterialsShape memory alloys, piezoelectric materials in sensors and actuators; self-healing coatingsMedical stents, actuators, smart structures; improved safetyCan reduce material use if designed correctly; production may use rare elements
NanotechnologyCarbon nanotubes in composites; nano-coatings for corrosion; improved semiconductorsBetter medical treatments; improved electronic devicesPotential environmental/health risks from nanoparticles (under research)
Internet of Things (IoT)Connected factory (Industry 4.0); real-time monitoring; predictive maintenanceSmart homes; connected healthcare; convenienceReduced energy use through smart controls; e-waste from obsolete devices
CAD/CAMFaster design; fewer prototypes needed; seamless design-to-manufacture; reduced errorsEnables smaller teams to design complex products; accessible design toolsReduces material waste; reduces transport of prototypes

Impact of Engineering Industries

✅ Positive Impacts
  • Creates employment and economic growth
  • Develops infrastructure (roads, bridges, utilities)
  • Improves quality of life (medical devices, clean water)
  • Drives innovation and technology advancement
  • Renewable energy industries reduce fossil fuel dependency
⚠️ Negative Impacts
  • CO₂ emissions from manufacturing and fossil fuel use
  • Noise and air pollution near industrial sites
  • Resource depletion — mining rare metals
  • Waste disposal — particularly electronics (e-waste)
  • Automation reduces employment in some sectors
  • Planned obsolescence creates unnecessary waste
3.6
Practical Engineering Skills & NEAWhat you must demonstrate in the practical project
NEA Structure

Your NEA must include a product that integrates both mechanical AND electronic/electrical systems. You need: engineering drawings (mechanical + electrical), a working prototype, production plan, evidence of QC, and a test/evaluation report.

Engineering Design Process

1. Analyse the Brief
Identify user needs, constraints, success criteria
↓
2. Research
Existing solutions, materials, components, standards
↓
3. Generate Ideas
Sketches, models, block diagrams, system ideas
↓
4. Develop & Model
CAD, circuit simulation, calculations, justification
↓
5. Plan Production
Materials, tools, processes, sequence, H&S, QC
↓
6. Manufacture
Make prototype, apply QC throughout, photograph stages
↓
7. Test & Evaluate
Against specification, suggest improvements, be honest
3.6D
Engineering DrawingsOrthographic, isometric, assembly, schematics
You MUST know these drawing types

3rd Angle Orthographic · Isometric 3D · Assembly Drawing · Section View · Electrical/Electronic Schematic · PCB Layout · Pneumatic Schematic

DIAGRAM — Third Angle Orthographic Projection

Key Drawing Conventions

ConventionMeaning / Use
Continuous thick lineVisible edges of object
Dashed thin lineHidden edges (not visible)
Chain (dot-dash) lineCentre lines — axes of symmetry or rotation
Dimension line + arrowheadsShows size of feature; text above line
Hatching (section)Cut surface in section view — diagonal lines
Title blockName, scale, date, material, drawing number, projection symbol

Isometric Drawing

DIAGRAM — Isometric View

CAD & CAM

2D CAD

Circuit diagrams, PCB layouts, orthographic drawings. Software: AutoCAD, LibreCAD. Used for engineering drawings with precise dimensions.

3D CAD

Solid modelling, isometric views, assemblies. Software: Fusion 360, SolidWorks, FreeCAD. Can generate 2D drawing views automatically.

CAM

Computer Aided Manufacture — CAD file drives machine. Laser cutting, CNC milling/routing, PCB drilling, vinyl cutting, 3D printing. G-code controls machine movement.

CNC

Computer Numerical Control — machine runs program (G-code). Lathe, mill, router. Repeatable to ±0.01mm. Reduces human error. Student can edit G/M codes for credit in NEA.

GL
Key Terms GlossaryQuick reference for essential engineering vocabulary
TermDefinition
AlloyMixture of two or more metals (or metal + non-metal) to improve properties
AnnealingHeating and slow cooling to soften metal and remove internal stress
BrittlenessTendency to fracture suddenly without significant deformation
CADComputer Aided Design — using software to create engineering drawings
CAMComputer Aided Manufacture — using CAD files to drive machines
CompositeMaterial made from two+ distinct components; matrix + reinforcement
CNCComputer Numerical Control — automated machine controlled by code
Compressive strengthMaximum compressive stress before failure
DensityMass per unit volume (kg/m³)
DuctilityAbility to be drawn into wire without fracturing
ElasticityAbility to return to original shape after force removed
Factor of SafetyRatio of material strength to actual working stress — safety margin
FerrousContaining iron (e.g. steel, cast iron)
FeedbackOutput signal returned to input to correct and control a system
Flow chartDiagram using standard shapes to show sequence of steps/decisions
TermDefinition
Gear ratioRatio of driven teeth to driver teeth — determines speed change
HardnessResistance to surface indentation or scratching
HydraulicSystem using liquid under pressure to transmit force
MalleabilityAbility to be shaped by hammering/rolling without cracking
Mechanical AdvantageRatio of output force (load) to input force (effort)
MonocoqueStructure where the outer shell carries all loads
NDTNon-Destructive Testing — inspect without damaging the part
NEANon-Exam Assessment — the practical project (40% of GCSE)
Orthographic2D technical drawing showing multiple views of a 3D object
Planned obsolescenceIntentionally designing products to fail or become outdated
PneumaticSystem using compressed air to transmit force
PlasticityPermanent deformation without fracture
StrainDeformation relative to original length (δl/l) — no units
StressForce per unit cross-sectional area (F/A) — Pascals
ThermoplasticPolymer that can be reheated and reshaped multiple times
ThermosettingPolymer that sets permanently; cannot be remelted
ToleranceAcceptable range of variation from a nominal dimension (±)
ToughnessAbility to absorb energy without fracturing
Young's ModulusMeasure of stiffness: stress/strain in the elastic region (GPa)
🎯 Final Exam Tips

1. Always give examples when asked about "new technologies" or "impacts".
2. Know your formulae — rearrange them. I = V/R means V = IR and R = V/I.
3. In calculations, show your working — even partial marks matter. Include units always.
4. For extended questions, structure your answer: Describe → Explain → Evaluate (DEE).
5. Remember thermoset vs thermoplastic is almost always tested. Thermoset = cannot re-melt.
6. Gear ratio: if driven > driver → output is SLOWER but more TORQUE. If driver > driven → FASTER, less torque.
7. NDT keeps components usable; destructive testing destroys them — know when each is appropriate.
8. Factor of Safety must be greater than 1 — higher for critical structures (bridges, aircraft).

AQA GCSE Engineering 8852 — Complete Revision Guide
Based on the official AQA specification (first exams 2019 onwards). Always check aqa.org.uk for updates.
